content:
# Disagreement Log — Joshua 6
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

This log records all verses in Joshua Chapter 6 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) meaningfully diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 6:1 — "shutting and shut up" vs. "shut itself up tight...gates were barred"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "shutting and-shut-up from-before the sons of Israel" | Preserves Hebrew double participle (sogereth u-mesuggereth) literally |
| LOGOS | "shutting and shut up before the sons of Israel" | Same construction, smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "shut itself up tight...its gates were barred" | Splits doubling into two English phrases; adds "gates" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS rendering. The Hebrew sogereth u-mesuggereth is a single paired construction using active and passive participles of the same root s-g-r, conveying both the active effort and the passive completeness of Jericho's closure. Splitting it into two separate clauses (PNEUMA) loses this unity. PNEUMA's addition of "gates" imports a word not present in the Hebrew of this verse. The consensus preserves the doubling as "shutting and shut up."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Not theologically significant — a matter of Hebrew structural fidelity.

---

## 6:2 — "I have given" vs. "I have already handed over"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I-have-given into-your-hand Jericho and-its-king, mighty-men of-valor" | Prophetic perfect preserved literally |
| LOGOS | "I have given into your hand Jericho, and its king, and the mighty men of valor" | Same; treats three items as distinct objects |
| PNEUMA | "I have already handed Jericho over to you: its king, its warriors, all of it" | Adds "already"; paraphrases natan as "handed over"; restructures list |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS core rendering. The English perfect tense "I have given" already communicates the completed-action sense of the prophetic perfect without the addition of "already." PNEUMA's "handed over" loses the directness of natan. PNEUMA's restructuring of the list ("its king, its warriors, all of it") departs significantly from the Hebrew syntax.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Not theologically significant, though the prophetic perfect is a notable rhetorical feature.

---

## 6:3 — "going around" vs. "circling" vs. "march around"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "going-around the-city one time" | Literal participial form of s-b-b |
| LOGOS | "circling the city one time" | English participial equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "March around the city...one circuit a day" | Dynamic; adds "circuit a day" not in Hebrew |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "circling the city one time" (LOGOS). "Going around" is literal but stilted in English. PNEUMA's "march around" imports a military connotation the Hebrew s-b-b does not carry, and "one circuit a day" adds interpretive material not present in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:5 — "beneath it" vs. "down in its place" vs. "collapse where it stands" (tachteyha)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-wall of-the-city shall-fall beneath-it" | Literal rendering of tachteyha |
| LOGOS | "the wall of the city shall fall down in its place" | Interpretive paraphrase |
| PNEUMA | "The city wall will collapse where it stands" | Dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the wall of the city shall fall beneath it" (EMET). The Hebrew tachteyha means "under her" (feminine suffix referring to the wall). The wall collapses downward in its own location. "Beneath it" is the most faithful rendering. LOGOS's "down in its place" and PNEUMA's "where it stands" are interpretively correct but paraphrastic. This same expression recurs in verse 20, and the consensus renders it identically in both places.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Three-way divergence. Not theologically significant but archaeologically noteworthy.

---

## 6:5 — "at the drawing out of the horn" vs. "when they make a long blast" vs. "when they sound the long blast"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "at-[the]-drawing-out of-[the]-horn of-the-ram" | Preserves infinitive construct of mashakh literally |
| LOGOS | "when they make a long blast with the horn of the ram" | Renders the action described by mashakh |
| PNEUMA | "when they sound the long blast on the ram's horn" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a synthesis: "when they draw out the horn of the ram." This preserves the verb mashakh ("to draw, extend") which denotes the prolonged blast (teki'ah gedolah) while reading naturally. EMET's rendering is overly literal; LOGOS and PNEUMA both lose the specific verb.

**Status:** Resolved by synthesis. Three-way divergence. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:6 — "Carry" vs. "Take up" vs. "Lift"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Carry the ark of the covenant" | Hebrew s-'-w as ongoing bearing |
| LOGOS | "Take up the ark of the covenant" | Inchoative — begin carrying |
| PNEUMA | "Lift the ark of the covenant" | Simple physical action |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Take up" (LOGOS). In a command context, the action is initiatory — Joshua is ordering the priests to begin carrying the ark, not describing an ongoing state. "Take up" captures the inception of the bearing.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:7 — "the armed one" (collective singular) vs. "the armed men" (plural)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-armed-one shall-pass-over" | Preserves Hebrew singular (ha-mechallets, collective) |
| LOGOS | "let the armed men pass on" | Plural; standard English collective rendering |
| PNEUMA | "The armed men will go ahead" | Dynamic plural |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "let the armed men pass on" (LOGOS). The Hebrew uses a singular participle as a collective — a common Hebrew construction. English handles collectives with plurals. The jussive "let...pass on" preserves the command form of the Hebrew.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant — a grammatical convention difference between Hebrew and English.

---

## 6:10 — Word order and phrasing of the silence command

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And-the-people, Joshua commanded, saying, 'You-shall-not shout...'" | Preserves Hebrew fronted object (ha-'am as topic) |
| LOGOS | "And Joshua commanded the people, saying, 'You shall not shout...'" | Standard English word order |
| PNEUMA | "But Joshua had given the people a strict command: 'Do not shout...'" | Dynamic; adds "strict"; changes tense to pluperfect |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS word order. The Hebrew topic-fronting is a discourse-level device that does not require preservation in English. PNEUMA's "strict" is interpretive addition, and the pluperfect tense ("had given") alters the narrative sequence of the Hebrew wayyiqtol chain.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:11 — "he caused to go around" (Hiphil) vs. "the ark went around" (smoothed)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And-he-caused-to-go-around [the]-ark of-YHWH the-city" | Preserves Hiphil causative with Joshua as subject |
| LOGOS | "And the ark of YHWH went around the city" | Smooths to intransitive; ark as subject |
| PNEUMA | "So the ark of YHWH went around the city" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS/PNEUMA. The Hebrew Hiphil (va-yassev) technically has Joshua as the implied subject causing the ark to circuit the city. But "he caused to go around" is unnatural in English, and the effective meaning — the ark went around the city — is what LOGOS and PNEUMA convey. The causative nuance is noted but not preserved in the consensus text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Not theologically significant, though the Hiphil hints at Joshua's agency.

---

## 6:13 — Infinitive absolute rendering (halokh ve-taqo'a)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "going, going and-blowing on-the-trumpets" | Doubles "going" for infinitive absolute |
| LOGOS | "going on continually, and blowing on the trumpets" | "Continually" captures ongoing aspect |
| PNEUMA | "blowing as they went...always marching, always blowing" | Dynamic repetition with "always" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The infinitive absolute halokh paired with ve-taqo'a expresses continuous simultaneous action. LOGOS's "going on continually, and blowing" captures this without EMET's awkward doubling or PNEUMA's interpretive restructuring. The infinitive absolute is one of Hebrew's most distinctive constructions, and "continually" serves as a functional English marker of the ongoing-action sense.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Three-way divergence. Not theologically significant — a matter of Hebrew grammatical rendering.

---

## 6:15 — "at the ascending of the dawn" vs. "at dawn"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "as-[the]-going-up of-the-dawn" | Literal rendering of ka'alot hashachar |
| LOGOS | "at the ascending of the dawn" | Preserves the verbal noun with smoother English |
| PNEUMA | "at dawn" | Condensed; loses the imagery |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew ka'alot hashachar ("at the going up / ascending of the dawn") is a vivid temporal expression — dawn as something that rises. PNEUMA's "at dawn" is accurate but loses the imagery entirely.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant but preserves Hebrew poetic register.

---

## 6:17 — "harlot" vs. "prostitute" for zonah

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-harlot" | Traditional biblical English |
| LOGOS | "the prostitute" | Modern English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "the prostitute" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "harlot" (EMET). Both terms are accurate for zonah. "Harlot" is the established rendering in the English Bible tradition (KJV, RSV, NASB) and maintains the register appropriate to the SOPHIA Bible. This is a two-against-one decision favoring EMET on grounds of register consistency.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Not theologically significant — a matter of English register.

---

## 6:17 — "devoted to destruction" vs. "under the ban — devoted to YHWH for destruction" for cherem

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "devoted-[to-destruction]" | Bracketed expansion of cherem |
| LOGOS | "devoted to destruction" | Clean rendering |
| PNEUMA | "under the ban — devoted to YHWH for destruction" | Double explanation with gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "devoted to destruction" (LOGOS). This captures both the sacral and destructive dimensions of cherem in a single phrase. EMET's brackets are a legitimate literal-translation convention but interrupt the reading. PNEUMA's "under the ban" adds a second term not in the Hebrew.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant in the strict sense, but cherem is a theologically weighty concept.

---

## 6:18 — Phrasing of the cherem contagion warning

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you-set the-camp of-Israel for-a-devoted-thing, and-you-trouble it" | Literal; preserves cherem terminology |
| LOGOS | "make the camp of Israel devoted to destruction and bring trouble upon it" | Smooth; consistent cherem vocabulary |
| PNEUMA | "bring the ban down on the camp of Israel itself — and you will bring disaster on it" | Dynamic; "ban" for cherem; "disaster" for 'akhar |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Maintains "devoted to destruction" consistently as the rendering of cherem throughout the chapter. PNEUMA's "ban" is a useful shorthand but inconsistent with the rendering chosen at verse 17.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:19 — Sentence structure and verb tense

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "are holy to-YHWH; into [the]-treasury of-YHWH they-shall-come" | Preserves Hebrew clause order |
| LOGOS | "it is holy to YHWH; it shall come into the treasury of YHWH" | Singular pronouns |
| PNEUMA | "are holy — set apart for YHWH. They go into YHWH's treasury" | Adds "set apart"; present tense |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET's structure with LOGOS's smoothness: "are holy to YHWH; into the treasury of YHWH they shall come." The plural predication matches the plural subject. PNEUMA's "set apart" is an interpretive gloss. The Hebrew clause order is preserved.

**Status:** Resolved by synthesis. Three-way divergence. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:20 — "beneath it" vs. "down in its place" vs. "where it stood" (reprise)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-wall fell beneath-it" | Literal tachteyha (same as v. 5) |
| LOGOS | "the wall fell down in its place" | Paraphrase (same as v. 5) |
| PNEUMA | "the wall collapsed where it stood" | Dynamic (same as v. 5) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the wall fell beneath it" (EMET), consistent with verse 5. The same Hebrew expression demands the same English rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET, consistent with v. 5.

---

## 6:21 — "with the mouth of the sword" vs. "with the edge of the sword" vs. "put to the sword"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "with-[the]-mouth of-[the]-sword" | Literal lefi-cherev |
| LOGOS | "with the edge of the sword" | Standard English equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "put to the sword" | Idiomatic English |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with the edge of the sword" (LOGOS). The Hebrew lefi-cherev ("at the mouth of the sword") is an idiom in which the sword is metaphorically depicted as devouring. "Edge of the sword" is the established English equivalent that preserves the prepositional syntax. PNEUMA's "put to the sword" loses the Hebrew structure entirely.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:21 — Merism preservation vs. smoothing

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from-man and-unto woman, from-young and-unto old" | Full merism structure preserved |
| LOGOS | "from man and unto woman, from young and unto old" | Same structure |
| PNEUMA | "men and women, young and old" | Merisms smoothed to simple lists |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS. The repeated min...ve-'ad ("from...and unto") pattern is a deliberate Hebrew rhetorical device expressing totality through paired extremes. It is comprehensible in English and must be preserved.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Not theologically significant but structurally important.

---

## 6:22 — "spying out" vs. "scouted" for meraggelim

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the-ones-spying-out the-land" | Literal participle |
| LOGOS | "who had spied out the land" | Relative clause, same root |
| PNEUMA | "who had scouted the land" | "Scouted" for r-g-l |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "who had spied out the land" (LOGOS). The Hebrew root r-g-l specifically denotes spying/reconnoitering. "Scouted" is a looser equivalent that loses the espionage connotation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:25 — "she dwelt" vs. "she has dwelt" vs. "she has lived"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "she-dwelt in-[the]-midst of-Israel unto this day" | Simple past tense |
| LOGOS | "she has dwelt in the midst of Israel unto this day" | Present perfect |
| PNEUMA | "she has lived among Israel to this day" | Present perfect; "lived" for yashav |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "she has dwelt" (LOGOS). The present perfect captures the Hebrew construction (qatal + 'ad hayyom hazzeh) which indicates an ongoing state reaching to the narrator's present. "Dwelt" preserves the specific verb yashav over the more generic "lived."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant, though the retrospective marker "unto this day" is a narratological feature relevant to the dating of Joshua's composition.

---

## 6:26 — "adjured" vs. "swore an oath" vs. "pronounced this oath"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "adjured [them] at-that time" | Hiphil of sh-b-' as "adjure" |
| LOGOS | "swore an oath at that time" | Hiphil rendered as oath-taking |
| PNEUMA | "pronounced this oath" | Dynamic rendering |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "swore an oath" (LOGOS). In this context, Joshua is the one pronouncing the oath/curse — he is not causing others to swear. "Swore an oath" is the most natural English rendering of the Hiphil in this context. EMET's "adjured" implies putting others under oath, which is not quite the sense here.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:26 — "doors" vs. "gates" for delatot

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "its-doors" | Literal delatot |
| LOGOS | "its gates" | City context = gates |
| PNEUMA | "its gates" | Same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "its gates" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). The Hebrew delatot can refer to doors or gates. In the context of a city's fortifications, "gates" is the correct English referent. The curse concerns the rebuilding of a fortified city — its foundation and its gates, the first and last elements of construction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA. Not theologically significant.

---

## 6:26 — "with his firstborn" vs. "at the cost of his firstborn"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "with-his-firstborn he-shall-lay-its-foundation" | Preserves ambiguous preposition bet |
| LOGOS | "With his firstborn he shall lay its foundation" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "at the cost of his firstborn...at the cost of his youngest son" | Makes bet explicit as price; adds "son" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS. The preposition bet carries multiple senses: "with," "at the cost of," "by means of." The SOPHIA Bible preserves the Hebrew ambiguity rather than resolving it for the reader. PNEUMA's "at the cost of" is the most likely meaning (confirmed by 1 Kings 16:34), but it narrows the text. PNEUMA also adds "son" after "youngest," which is not in the Hebrew (the Hebrew reads tse'iro, "his youngest," without specifying "son").

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Not theologically significant in isolation, but the curse and its fulfillment form an important intertextual arc.

---

## 6:27 — "fame was in all the land" vs. "fame spread through the whole land"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his-fame was in-all the-land" | Literal stative: shom'o hayah bekhol-ha'arets |
| LOGOS | "his fame was in all the land" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "his fame spread through the whole land" | Dynamic; adds motion verb "spread" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS. The Hebrew uses the stative verb hayah ("was"), not a verb of spreading or motion. PNEUMA's "spread" imports a dynamic not present in the text. The stative phrasing is both accurate and effective in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Not theologically significant.

---

## Summary of Resolutions

| Verse | Divergence | Resolution | Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6:1 | Double participle rendering | EMET/LOGOS | Hebrew structural fidelity |
| 6:2 | "I have given" vs. "already handed over" | EMET/LOGOS | Prophetic perfect; no addition needed |
| 6:3 | "going around" / "circling" / "march around" | LOGOS | Natural English without military import |
| 6:5 | tachteyha rendering | EMET | Literal accuracy |
| 6:5 | mashakh rendering | Synthesis | Preserve verb while smoothing |
| 6:6 | "Carry" / "Take up" / "Lift" | LOGOS | Inchoative sense |
| 6:7 | Collective singular vs. plural | LOGOS | English collective convention |
| 6:10 | Word order; "strict" addition | LOGOS | Standard English; no additions |
| 6:11 | Hiphil causative vs. smoothed | LOGOS/PNEUMA | Readability |
| 6:13 | Infinitive absolute rendering | LOGOS | "Continually" for ongoing action |
| 6:15 | "ascending of the dawn" vs. "at dawn" | LOGOS | Preserve imagery |
| 6:17 | "harlot" vs. "prostitute" | EMET | Biblical English register |
| 6:17 | cherem rendering | LOGOS | "Devoted to destruction" — clean |
| 6:18 | cherem warning phrasing | LOGOS | Consistency with v. 17 |
| 6:19 | Sentence structure | Synthesis | Plural predication; Hebrew order |
| 6:20 | tachteyha (reprise) | EMET | Consistency with v. 5 |
| 6:21 | lefi-cherev rendering | LOGOS | Standard English idiom |
| 6:21 | Merism preservation | EMET/LOGOS | Hebrew rhetorical structure |
| 6:22 | "spied out" vs. "scouted" | LOGOS | Root r-g-l precision |
| 6:25 | "dwelt" vs. "lived"; verb tense | LOGOS | Present perfect; specific verb |
| 6:26 | "adjured" vs. "swore an oath" | LOGOS | Context-appropriate rendering |
| 6:26 | "doors" vs. "gates" | LOGOS/PNEUMA | City fortification context |
| 6:26 | "with" vs. "at the cost of" | EMET/LOGOS | Preserve Hebrew ambiguity |
| 6:27 | "was" vs. "spread" | EMET/LOGOS | Hebrew stative verb |

**Resolution tally:**
- LOGOS alone: 13
- EMET/LOGOS: 6
- LOGOS/PNEUMA: 2
- EMET alone: 2
- Synthesis: 2
- Total disagreements: 25

**Theological significance:** None of the disagreements in Joshua 6 rise to the level of theological significance. All are matters of translation philosophy, English register, or Hebrew structural fidelity. The three translators are in full agreement on the meaning of the text; they differ only in how to express that meaning in English.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA.*
*The SOPHIA Bible — Wisdom in consilience.*
